Cache Firebase Storage Images with Cloudflare Workers — Completely Free Bandwidth Savings

If you're using Firebase Storage to host blog images like I was, you've probably noticed bandwidth costs creeping up. Every time someone visits an article, the images load straight from Firebase Storage, and all of that bandwidth lands on your Firebase bill. The more articles and readers you have, the worse the bandwidth bill gets.
Is there a way to put a cache layer in front so images don't get fetched from Firebase Storage every time? Yes — and
. This post walks through how to use
with a custom subdomain to cache Firebase Storage images, letting Cloudflare's global CDN absorb most of the traffic.
Before getting into the implementation, let's cover the principle. Otherwise you won't know why it works.
In short, we're going to put Cloudflare in front of Firebase Storage as a "middleman" that caches the images:
language-txt shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast

User → images.yourdomain.com → Cloudflare CDN (cache layer) → Firebase Storage
                                      ↑
                              If cached, return directly — never touch Firebase
User → images.yourdomain.com → Cloudflare CDN (cache layer) → Firebase Storage
If cached, return directly — never touch Firebase
The first time someone requests an image, Cloudflare fetches it from Firebase Storage and stashes a copy at the edge. Every subsequent request goes straight from the edge —
, which means no bandwidth charge on the Firebase side.
Before we go any further, let me cover the cost — that's probably what most people care about anyway:
All of it free. Cloudflare is one of the few CDNs that doesn't bill on bandwidth, and people have run 5–9 TB/month on the free plan without trouble.
And once a request hits cache, it doesn't go through the Worker at all — so it doesn't consume your daily 100k Worker invocation quota either. For blog images, this is more than enough.
The one requirement is that you own a domain and host its DNS with Cloudflare (which is also free).
Watch out for the paid traps
The Workers free plan doesn't require a credit card, but the dashboard has features (Durable Objects, Workers AI, etc.) that might nudge you toward the $5/month paid plan.
— we only need basic Worker functionality, none of those advanced features.
You might be thinking: "why not just use
Simple — if your main domain is already running a blog or another project, the paths can collide easily. A subdomain (like
) is fully isolated and won't interfere with anything on the main site. And the final image URL ends up looking like:
Way cleaner than the long Firebase Storage URL.
Now that we've covered the principle and cost, on to the implementation.
Step 1: Host your domain DNS on Cloudflare
If your domain isn't on Cloudflare yet:
At your domain registrar, change nameservers to the ones Cloudflare gave you.
Back in Cloudflare, click confirm, wait for DNS to propagate (usually minutes to hours).
If your domain's already on Cloudflare, skip to Step 2.
Delete the default code and paste this:

// Build the Firebase Storage URL from the path
with your actual Firebase project name, and
In case you're wondering what's actually happening — three things:
: checks the request origin and only allows your own domain and local dev. Stops other people from hotlinking your image proxy.
: builds the Firebase Storage URL from the request path, fetches from Firebase.
: adds cache-control headers on the response, telling the Cloudflare CDN and the browser to cache the image for a year.
entry exists so local development still shows images. Since we're using
for matching, any port (4000, 3000, 8080…) works fine.
Cloudflare creates the DNS record for you, click confirm.
Because our Worker requests Firebase Storage without a token, the images need to be publicly readable.
for public read — other paths are unaffected.

You might be worried about the security implications, but consider: these images were already public via blog articles — anyone could see them. The original token was sitting in your HTML for anyone who opens devtools to grab anyway. Switching to public read doesn't actually add any new risk.
Open a browser and type in:
If you see the image, you're good.
Now you need to replace all the Firebase Storage image URLs in your blog with the new format.
If you have lots of articles, doing it by hand isn't realistic. Bulk replace with
language-bash shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast

# Step 1: replace domain and strip token
grep -rl "firebasestorage\.googleapis\.com/v0/b/YOUR_PROJECT\.appspot\.com" source/ | \
while IFS= read -r f; do
  sed -i '' 's|https://firebasestorage\.googleapis\.com/v0/b/YOUR_PROJECT\.appspot\.com/o/\([^?]*\)?alt=media&token=[a-f0-9\-]*|https://images.yourdomain.com/\1|g' "$f"
done

# Step 2: replace %2F with /
for i in $(seq 1 5); do
  grep -rl "images\.yourdomain\.com/[^)]*%2F" source/ 2>/dev/null | \
  while IFS= read -r f; do
    sed -i '' 's|\(images\.yourdomain\.com/[^)]*\)%2F|\1/|g' "$f"
  done
done
# Step 1: replace domain and strip token
Step 2 needs to run multiple times because

per pass — the loop ensures all of them get converted. Also, if you have images from multiple Firebase projects, double-check you're only replacing URLs from the target project so you don't accidentally touch other projects' image URLs.

How do you get URLs for new images going forward? The rule is just:
https://images.yourdomain.com/ + the path in Firebase Storage

Last bit: let's cover how the caching actually flows, so you can feel more confident about the setup.
The Worker sets two key headers:

tells Cloudflare's edge nodes: "cache this for a year". On the first request, Cloudflare fetches from Firebase and stores a copy at the edge closest to the user. After that, anyone requesting the same image gets it straight from the edge —
no Worker invocation, no Firebase hit
tells the browser: "cache this locally for a year, and it won't change". So when the same user visits the same post again, the browser uses its local copy —

User → Cloudflare edge (no cache) → Worker → Firebase Storage
                ↓
        Store copy at edge + return to user
User → Cloudflare edge (no cache) → Worker → Firebase Storage
Store copy at edge + return to user
User → browser local cache → render directly
Since blog images don't change after upload, setting a 1-year cache with
With Cloudflare Workers + CDN caching, you can dramatically cut Firebase Storage bandwidth — for $0. Setup doesn't take much time, but the savings are long-term.
: no credit card, no paid plan needed.
: Cloudflare has 300+ edge nodes worldwide; images come from the closest one.
: cache hits never touch Firebase, bandwidth goes to zero.
: only your own site can access — stops hotlinking.
: from long Firebase URLs to clean subdomain paths.
If you're also using Firebase Storage for images, this approach is worth trying.

If you're using Firebase Storage to host blog images like I was, you've probably noticed bandwidth costs creeping up — every article view pulls the images straight from Firebase Storage, and all of it lands on your Firebase bill. This post walks through how to use Cloudflare Workers plus a custom subdomain to cache those images. The whole setup is free and doesn't require a credit card.
